C001.claim: Nature extremizes key quantities such as action in classical mechanics and expectation value of energy in quantum systems.

results.title: History of variational principles in physics - Wikipedia
results.snippet: 1 week ago - Variational methods are exploited in many modern software applications to simulate matter and light. Since the development of analytical mechanics in the 18th century, the fundamental equations of physics can be expressed in terms of action principles, where the variational principle is applied to the action of a system in order to recover the fundamental equation of motion.

results.title: Variational principle - Wikipedia
results.snippet: 1 week ago - A variational principle is a mathematical procedure that renders a physical problem solvable by the calculus of variations, which concerns finding functions that optimize the values of quantities that depend on those functions. For example, the problem of determining the shape of a hanging ...

results.title: Variational method (quantum mechanics) - Wikipedia
results.snippet: November 21, 2025 - The variational principle formulated above is the basis of the variational method used in quantum mechanics and quantum chemistry to find approximations to the ground state.

results.title: Unified field theory - Wikipedia
results.snippet: 2 weeks ago - These and other models of electromagnetism and gravity were pursued by Albert Einstein in his attempts at a classical unified field theory. By 1930 Einstein had already considered the Einstein-Maxwell–Dirac System [Dongen]. This system is (heuristically) the super-classical [Varadarajan] limit of (the not mathematically well-defined) quantum electrodynamics. One can extend this system to include the weak and strong nuclear forces to get the Einstein–Yang-Mills–Dirac System. The French physicist Marie-Antoinette Tonnelat published a paper in the early 1940s on the standard commutation relations for the quantized spin-2 field, in an attempt to unify general relativity with quantum mechanics.

results.title: Unification of theories in physics - Wikipedia
results.snippet: February 22, 2026 - Unification of theories about observable fundamental phenomena of nature is one of the primary goals of physics. Isaac Newton’s unification of terrestrial and celestial mechanics into one theory of gravity in the 17th century and James Clerk Maxwell’s unification of electricity with magnetism ...

results.title: Variational Principles of Physics and the Infinite Ramsey Theory
results.snippet: The synthesis of variational principles and Infinite Ramsey Theory redefines classical and quantum mechanics frameworks. Hamilton's Least Action Principle establishes stationary action for actual trajectories in configurational space. Infinite Ramsey Theorem confirms the existence of monochromatic cliques in bi-colored graphs of actual and trial paths. Maupertuis's principle and its quantum ...

results.title: Variational Principles in Physical Systems - Trust Atoms
results.snippet: Variational principles explain how physical systems optimize action, time, and energy. Learn how least action unifies classical mechanics, relativity, optics, and quantum physics.

results.title: Lagrangian and Hamiltonian Mechanics - Durham
results.snippet: As a consequence, many of the concepts used in the formulation of quantum mechanics already make an appearance in the Lagrangian and Hamiltonian frameworks. Additionally, understanding the classical solutions for a given quantum mechanical system can often serve as a first step towards its full quantum mechanical solution.

results.title: A Time-Symmetric Variational Formulation of Quantum Mechanics ...
results.snippet: Standard quantum mechanics requires two distinct dynamical principles: unitary evolution between measurements and stochastic collapse during measurement. By recasting the theory as a boundary-value problem on the space of histories, we replace this dualism with a single variational principle.

results.title: Calculus of variations - Wikipedia
results.snippet: The calculus of variations (or variational calculus) is a field of mathematical analysis that uses variations, which are small changes in functions and functionals, to find maxima and minima of functionals: mappings from a set of functions to the real numbers.

results.title: Variational and Extremum Principles in Macroscopic Systems
results.snippet: The main goal of Variational and Extremum Principles in Macroscopic Systems is to collect various mathematical formulations and examples of physical reasoning that involve both basic theoretical aspects and applications of variational and extremum approaches to systems of the macroscopic world.

results.title: Variational method (quantum mechanics) - Wikipedia
results.snippet: November 21, 2025 - Each electron can be thought to see the nuclear charge partially "shielded" by the other electron, so we can use a trial wavefunction equal with an "effective" nuclear charge Z < 2: The expectation value of H in this state is: ... This is minimal for Z = 27/16 implying shielding reduces the effective charge to ~1.69. Substituting this value of Z into the expression for H yields 729E1/128 = −77.5 eV, within 2% of the experimental value, −78.975 eV. Even closer estimations of this energy have been found using more complicated trial wave functions with more parameters. This is done in physical chemistry via variational Monte Carlo.

[1] Variational method (quantum mechanics) - Wikipedia
URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Variational_method_(quantum_mechanics)
Snippet: November 21, 2025 - Each electron can be thought to see the nuclear charge partially "shielded" by the other electron, so we can use a trial wavefunction equal with an "effective" nuclear charge Z < 2: The expectation value of H in this state is: ... This is minimal for Z = 27/16 implying shielding reduces the effective charge to ~1.69. Substituting this value of Z into the expression for H yields 729E1/128 = −77.5 eV, within 2% of the experimental value, −78.975 eV. Even closer estimations of this energy have been found using more complicated trial wave functions with more parameters. This is done in physical chemistry via variational Monte Carlo.

C006.claim: Light takes the path of least time, explaining refraction via Fermat's principle as a variational outcome.

results.title: Fermat's principle of least time - MIT
results.snippet: Fermat's principle of least time Fermat's principle of least time states that light from a point source takes the path to a destination which takes the least total time. If we place an obstacle in a path that is not the shortest, the brightness at the destination will be negligibly affected.

results.title: Refraction of Light: Principle, Laws, Applications, vs. Reflection
results.snippet: Rather, it behaves according to Fermat's principle which states that light always wants to minimize time of travelling and chooses the path that takes less time.

results.title: Fermat's Principle Explained - numberanalytics.com
results.snippet: Fermat's Principle was first proposed by Pierre de Fermat in 1662, as a way to explain the law of refraction. Fermat was a French mathematician who made significant contributions to the field of optics and mathematics.

results.title: Snell's Law And Fermat's Principle: Uniting Light's Path And Speed
results.snippet: Fermat's Time Principle vs. Snell's Angle Relation Snell's Law and Fermat's Principle are two fundamental concepts in optics that describe the behavior of light as it travels through different media. While Snell's Law provides a mathematical relationship between the angles of incidence and refraction, Fermat's Principle offers a broader, more philosophical framework for understanding light's ...

results.title: State fermet principle and explain how it account for reflection ...
results.snippet: Fermat's Principle and its Applications Fermat's Principle, also known as the principle of least time, is a fundamental concept in optics that describes the path taken by a light ray between two points.
